t: Does not occur in the context of anorexia or bulimia; no body image disturbance.
Not better explained by another medical or mental condition (e.g., allergy, OCD).
NICE NG69 — specific intervention rules for ARFID.
Medical evaluation — weight, height, BMI, laboratory nutritional deficiency.
Exclusion of allergy and GI pathology.
Body image and weight fear are central.
Sensory sensitivity component; ARFID can be comorbid with ASD.
Specific object; multiple food types in ARFID.
Laborator: tam qan, ferritin, sink, vitamin D, B12, folat.
Multidisciplinary approach: nutritionist + CBT therapist + feeding-occupational therapist.
CBT-AR — Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for ARFID (Thomas J.J.) — manualized.
Stepped food exposure — gradual introduction of new food types.
Medical: nutritional correction; severe case enteral feeding.
SSRIs for comorbid anxiety; no FDA-approved specific medication for ARFID.
Thomas J.J. CBT-AR — the evidence-based approach.
CBT for ARFID (CBT-AR — CBT for ARFID) — Thomas (Thomas J.J.)
Manualized; protocols tailored to sensory, low interest, fear subtypes.
The role of the family in the exposure to food.
New food approach → touch → taste → swallow steps.
Nine-Item ARFID Screen (NIAS — Nine Item ARFID Screen)
With a multidisciplinary approach, improvement in most patients.
Long-term follow-up in the context of autism and OCD.
Clinical diagnosis requires significant functional impairment or nutritional deficiency.
Myth 2: "The patient can be forced to eat by starvation"
Hunger strategy ineffective in ARFID and harmful to family-child relationship; stepwise exposure recommended.
Myth 3: "ARFID is a 'mild form' of anorexia"
distinct clinical unit; body image disturbance and weight concern absent; intervention differs.
Myth 4: "ARFID on the autism spectrum cannot be treated"
sensory integration and behavioral approaches are also effective in the context of autism.
It can progress without treatment; early intervention prevents nutritional complications.
Thomas J.J., Eddy K.T. Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy for ARFID. Cambridge University Press; 2018.
Zickgraf H.F., Ellis J.M. Initial validation of the Nine Item Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder Screen (NIAS). Appetite 2018;123:32–42.
